sources.title: Konstantin Toropin and Matt O'Brien, "Anthropic CEO says it cannot 'accede' to Pentagon's demands for AI use," The Associated Press (2026).
sources.note: Reports on the dispute between Anthropic and the Pentagon over safeguards governing military use of Claude.

sources.title: National Academies of Sciences, Engineering, and Medicine, Convergence: Facilitating Transdisciplinary Integration of Life Sciences, Physical Sciences, Engineering, and Beyond (2014).
sources.note: A foundational account of how complex problems increasingly require integration across fields.

sources.title: OECD, Mission-Oriented Innovation.
sources.note: Explains how public institutions can convene actors around complex problems that do not yield to isolated interventions.

sources.title: Stanford Institute for Human-Centered Artificial Intelligence, 2025 AI Index Report: Policy and Governance.
sources.note: Tracks the growth of AI-related policy and regulatory activity.

sources.title: OECD, AI Principles, adopted in 2019 and updated in 2024.
sources.note: Sets out an international framework for trustworthy and human-centred AI.

sources.title: National Institute of Standards and Technology, Artificial Intelligence Risk Management Framework.
sources.note: Provides practical guidance for identifying and managing AI risks.

sources.title: European Commission, AI Act: The First-Ever Legal Framework on AI.
sources.note: Describes the European Union's risk-based legal framework for AI.

sources.title: National Institute of Standards and Technology, CHIPS for America.
sources.note: Connects semiconductor manufacturing with research, workforce, supply-chain resilience, and national competitiveness.

sources.title: The White House, National Strategy for the Arctic Region (2022).
sources.note: Links security, climate, economic development, Indigenous livelihoods, and international cooperation in the Arctic.

sources.title: NATO, Defence Innovation Accelerator for the North Atlantic (DIANA).
sources.note: Connects emerging technology with collective defence and security challenges.

sources.title: Cybersecurity and Infrastructure Security Agency, Joint Cyber Defense Collaborative AI Cybersecurity Collaboration Playbook (2025).
sources.note: Describes a government-industry mechanism for coordinating cybersecurity planning and response.

sources.title: U.S. Government Accountability Office, Fraud and Improper Payments: Data Quality and a Skilled Workforce Are Essential for Unlocking the Benefits of Artificial Intelligence (2025).
sources.note: Documents the federal shortage of AI expertise and hiring and compensation barriers.

sources.title: Stanford Institute for Human-Centered Artificial Intelligence, 2025 AI Index Report: Research and Development.
sources.note: Reports industry's share of notable AI model development in 2024.

sources.title: Ted Chiang, "What's Expected of Us," Nature (2005).
sources.note: The original Nature publication of Chiang's short story.

sources.title: Ted Chiang, "What's Expected of Us" (PDF).
sources.note: A PDF copy of Chiang's short story.

sources.title: T. M. Scanlon, What We Owe to Each Other.
sources.note: Publisher information for Scanlon's account of what people owe to one another.

sources.title: Stanford Encyclopedia of Philosophy, Contractualism.
sources.note: An overview of contractualism and Scanlon's account of justifiability to others.

preview.text: ), the story considers what happens when people come to believe that the future is already fixed and that their choices no longer matter. I am using it here less as an argument about free will than as a warning against the passive language that often surrounds technology.

body.text: ), the story considers what happens when people come to believe that the future is already fixed and that their choices no longer matter. I am using it here less as an argument about free will than as a warning against the passive language that often surrounds technology. We say that models will improve and governments will eventually adapt, as though these were natural events rather than the accumulated result of decisions made by companies, researchers, and institutions.
I am using this phrase from T. M. Scanlon's
What We Owe to Each Other

in a institutional sense. When technical and policy institutions jointly shape systems that affect public life, neither can treat the knowledge or responsibilities of the other as someone else's problem.

preview.text: centres on whether conduct can be justified to others while respecting their separate interests, rather than securing agreement or producing a preferred overall outcome.

body.text: centres on whether conduct can be justified to others while respecting their separate interests, rather than securing agreement or producing a preferred overall outcome. I borrow that distinction to claim that the worlds of policy and technology do not need to agree on everything, but the decisions they make together should be defensible to the people affected by them. They may not owe one another agreement, but they do owe one another enough openness to make their decisions defensible to the people who will live with them.
